text:
How safe is the heater? Will it burn or give off toxic fumes?
Have you considered building in a temperature sensor, maybe another strip with a high resistance temperature coefficient?

The finished product looks very much like the vivarium heat mats, except this runs at a low voltage.

author: @bradleyconroy4500
text: Nice use of its resistive properties.  You already did capacitors. You can make inductors by alternating layers of ink and fingernail polish or varnish.  If you can use your graphitic carbon nitride and maybe boron carbide as doping agents, you could do diodes, transistors etc...  Then you could 3d print entire circuits including the circuit board, battery and all of the components.

author: @ianmathieson65
text:
As always, an interesting and informative video.  To buy conductive inks or paints see, for example, http://www.aquadag.com/conductive-inks-coatings-27433.htm.

As an A-level student back in the late 1950’s I built a simple rig for scratching thin closely spaced lines in a coating of Aquadag which I painted onto a glass microscope slide and then air-dried.  I was creating a transmission diffraction grating which we then tested for performance in a physics class and used the generated diffraction pattern to calculate the line spacing.  It agreed very closely with the spacing I’d set the rig to produce.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice post mate - cheers

author: @cruzemissile5409
text: I've been trying to look up the best microwave receptor materials but it seems like that info is being censored here in the US by our internet just as a lot of other info seems to be filtered out of public knowledge .

replies.author: @everest3334
replies.text:
there's no such thing as the best as such, only for a given application and remember you can never really remove the infrared radiation totally given that the microwaves produced are external to the receptor materials but see http://www.che.iitm.ac.in/~srinivar/PrePrint/GraphiteMW.Chandrasekaran.2013.pdf as a starting point.

funny enough carbon nanofoam is the better option for insulation but it to will be affected by the external microwaves.

now how to make and form a carbon nanofoam raku kiln fired by microwave might  be an option as would how to make a tap capable of regulating the flow from the bottom of your receptacle directly into molds etc...

replies.author: @cruzemissile5409
replies.text: Thank You for your reply !!

author: @danajohnson3799
text: Would it work in reverse, that is to ask, would it generate current if heated?

replies.author: @zenmanproject
replies.text: Dana Johnson Maybe microwatts at best. To generate electricity from heat you need two different inks. This is a project I am actively working on. You need an n-type ink (ink intercalated with a compound that has a high negative charge) something like Phosphorus. I’m trying TiO2 at the moment. Then a p-type ink (ink intercalated with a compound with a high positive charge. I am going to try AlCl3 soon.

replies.author: @victorst5997
replies.text: He won't say what polymers he uses. It's always 'secret'. What's the point in "theaching' how to make a heater, if he won't say the formula to adhere a water based polymer on PET? All these videos are advertising.

replies.author: @zenmanproject
replies.text:
Victor ST He is selective about what he shares and what he keeps to himself. That’s fine with me. He has taught me enough to ask the right questions in google scholar and other professional Chemists. I have also reproduced many of his experiments in my workshop with very good success. 

Let’s be fair here. He certainly isn’t getting rich from youtube videos. If his videos can help him sell licenses to his secretive formulas so he can fund sharing the simpler formulas with us. That works for me!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: it won't mate - the ink i show how to make is meant for paper and adsorbent surfaces - if you want to try to adhere to plastics and non adsorbant surfaces you have to change the binder - polyurethane works well if you really want to get into it shellac and d-limonine are awesome - but xpensive and difficult to handle - as with all things experimentation is your best answer mate

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: That is quite a rude thing to say mate - especially when you think that on lots of other videos i go into all kinds of binders and how to make them and use them. The point is some folks don't want to make the ink - they want to make the heater - sme folks want to make the ink - for them there are other videos that teach that.

author: @stemcampprojects7951
text: Another great video - thanks for sorting out the audio!  I love the way you encourage experimentation.  I just posted a video about trying a modification of the carbonization technique you used on the carrot -- instead of zinc chloride, I used sodium chloride.  It is also a microwave susceptor, and seemed to work just fine, while being even less toxic than zinc chloride.  I'll be trying it more soon.

replies.author: @VinoVeritas_
replies.text: STEM Camp Projects Did you simply change out the zinc chloride for sodium chloride and follow the rest of Rob's carrot battery video? I'm going  to make a carbon from potato peelings today and give the sodium chloride a try. I have tried it with used teabags, but didn't get anywhere near the type of results Rob did with the carrot and zinc chloride battery.

replies.author: @stemcampprojects7951
replies.text: I haven't done that yet - I need to dry out some carrots and try it.  I plan on doing it.  I also have a bunch of other things to try to verify that it works in a similar enough manner.  Rob makes it look easy - but there's a lot of little things he does right without even thinking about it.  I tried making an ink, but my powder wasn't fine enough and I forgot to strain it through the cheesecloth.  Live and learn!

replies.author: @VinoVeritas_
replies.text: STEM Camp Projects I have found that the electrode material is a massive factor in my experiments. Whenever I use aluminium, I rarely get beyond 0.75v. However, when I've made aluminium-air cells, I've managed to hit 0.93v repeatedly. I achieve that with a choline chloride-urea deep eutectic solvent combined with carboxymethyl cellulose to make a gel. The gel acts as both the electrolyte and separator. I treat these as primary cells and as such don't charge them. The 0.93v is generated solely by the redox reaction between the aluminium and oxygen. I'm working on creating a simple led torch that uses scrap pieces of aluminium and could be distributed in developing countries. I have made a prototype that's smaller than a UK one pence coin and can light an led with the aid of a joule thief. I was able to use the tiny tab of aluminium that connects the ring pull to the main body of a drinks can. It must only be 9mm² (3x3mm) in size, but the light emitted is powerful enough to illuminate several lines of a book. I  was able to repeat this without using a joule thief, but required 4 of the cells to be connected in series. These tiny aluminium-air powered torches could be distributed in the concave base of an aluminium drinks can and would enable large companies to fulfil part of their socially responsible objectives.

replies.author: @stemcampprojects7951
replies.text: Very cool.  I love projects that turn unused items or trash into usable things.

replies.author: @zenmanproject
replies.text:
bashpr0mpt Nope. That’s incorrect. If you’re comparing apples to apples, i.e. two electric heating systems, this will theoretically be identical. 

From the US Department of Energy:

“Electric resistance heating is 100% energy efficient in the sense that all the incoming electric energy is converted to heat. However, most electricity is produced from coal, gas, or oil generators that convert only about 30% of the fuel's energy into electricity.”

In other words if you burn coal, turn it into electricity just to use the electricity to produce heat, that’s a waste of energy... just burn the coal. But if you’re comparing the heat of this element to the element in your toaster oven or space heater. They will produce roughly the same heat output for electrical input.
